s: In 2006, NASA accidentally sold a bag containing Moon dust from the Apollo missions at a government auction for just $995 — they later tried to get it back in court and lost.
q: How much did a bag of Apollo Moon dust accidentally sell for at a government auction?
e: Nancy Lee Carlson bought the bag at a US Marshals auction. When she sent it to NASA for authentication, the agency seized it and refused to return it. She sued and won — a federal judge ruled she was the rightful owner since the government had sold it to her. The bag later sold at Sotheby's for $1.8 million.

s: Titan, Saturn's largest moon, has such thick atmosphere and low gravity that a human with artificial wings could actually fly by flapping their arms.
q: On which moon could a human theoretically fly by flapping artificial wings?
e: Titan's atmosphere is 1.5 times denser than Earth's, and its surface gravity is only about 14% of Earth's (similar to the Moon). This combination means the force needed to stay airborne is dramatically reduced — a human with modest wing attachments could sustain flight with arm power alone.

s: The largest known structure in the universe is the Hercules-Corona Borealis Great Wall, a filament of galaxies stretching about 10 billion light-years across — so large it challenges cosmological models.
q: What is the largest known structure in the observable universe?
e: Discovered in 2013 through gamma-ray burst mapping, this structure is roughly 10% of the diameter of the observable universe. It challenges the cosmological principle, which states the universe should look roughly uniform at large scales. How such an enormous structure formed is still debated.

s: Venus has over 1,600 major volcanoes — more than any other planet in the solar system — though scientists still debate whether any are actively erupting today.
q: Which planet has the most known major volcanoes in the solar system?
e: Radar imaging by the Magellan spacecraft revealed Venus's surface is dominated by volcanic features. In 2023, analysis of Magellan data found a volcanic vent that appeared to change shape between orbits, providing the strongest evidence yet for ongoing volcanic activity.

s: The Wow! Signal of 1977 was a 72-second narrowband radio burst from space that was so startling the astronomer who found it wrote 'Wow!' in red pen on the printout — and it has never been satisfactorily explained.
q: How long did the famous Wow! Signal last?
e: Detected by the Big Ear radio telescope at Ohio State University, the signal came from the direction of Sagittarius and matched the expected profile of an interstellar communication. Despite decades of follow-up observations, the signal has never repeated. Hypotheses range from comets to alien transmissions.

s: If two pieces of the same type of metal touch in the vacuum of space, they permanently fuse together through a process called cold welding — no heat required.
q: What happens when two clean metal surfaces touch in space vacuum?
e: On Earth, metal surfaces have oxide layers and adsorbed gases that prevent this. In the vacuum of space, these barriers are absent, so when clean metal surfaces meet, atoms on each side cannot distinguish which piece they belong to and form metallic bonds instantly. Engineers must account for this when designing spacecraft mechanisms.

s: The Cassini spacecraft's Grand Finale saw it dive between Saturn and its innermost ring 22 times before deliberately plunging into Saturn's atmosphere to protect the potentially habitable moons from contamination.
q: Why did NASA deliberately crash Cassini into Saturn?
a: To protect potentially habitable moons from contamination
d: To collect more data about the rings
e: NASA chose to destroy Cassini rather than risk it eventually crashing into Enceladus or Titan and contaminating them with Earth microbes. During its final 22 orbits, Cassini gathered unprecedented data about Saturn's interior, gravity field, and ring composition in a region no spacecraft had ever explored.

s: There are at least 2 trillion galaxies in the observable universe — ten times more than previously thought, according to a 2016 Hubble-based study.
q: Approximately how many galaxies are estimated to be in the observable universe?
e: By analyzing deep-field images and correcting for galaxies too faint to observe with current technology, astronomers at the University of Nottingham estimated 2 trillion galaxies. About 90% of these are too faint and distant for current telescopes, meaning we have directly observed less than 10% of what exists.

s: Uranus spins on its side — its axial tilt is about 98 degrees, likely the result of a collision with an Earth-sized object billions of years ago.
q: What is unusual about Uranus's axial tilt?
a: It spins nearly on its side at about 98 degrees
e: This extreme tilt means that during its 84-year orbit, each pole gets about 42 years of continuous sunlight followed by 42 years of darkness. The collision hypothesis also explains why Uranus's moons and rings orbit in the same tilted plane.

s: Neutron stars are so dense that a sugar-cube-sized piece would weigh about 1 billion tons — roughly the weight of Mount Everest.
q: How much would a sugar-cube-sized piece of a neutron star weigh?
e: When massive stars collapse, protons and electrons are crushed together to form neutrons. The resulting neutron star packs 1-2 solar masses into a sphere just 20 km across. A single tablespoon of neutron star material outweighs all the humans on Earth combined.

s: The Pale Blue Dot photograph, taken by Voyager 1 in 1990 from 6 billion km away, shows Earth as a tiny speck less than a single pixel across — inspiring Carl Sagan's famous reflection on humanity's place in the cosmos.
q: From how far away was the famous Pale Blue Dot photograph taken?
e: Sagan convinced NASA to turn Voyager's camera around for one last look at Earth. The resulting image showed our planet as a faint dot suspended in a sunbeam. His accompanying essay, beginning 'Look again at that dot,' is considered one of the most profound pieces of space-age writing.

s: The universe has no center — the Big Bang did not happen at a single point in space. Instead, it happened everywhere simultaneously, and all of space has been expanding ever since.
q: Where did the Big Bang happen?
d: At a specific location in space
At the edge of the universe
e: A common misconception is that the Big Bang was an explosion from a central point. In reality, all of space was compressed to extreme density and began expanding everywhere at once. Every point in the universe can equally claim to be the 'center' — or equivalently, none can.

s: Saturn's moon Mimas looks almost identical to the Death Star from Star Wars — it has a massive impact crater called Herschel that is nearly one-third of the moon's entire diameter.
q: Which of Saturn's moons resembles the Death Star due to its giant impact crater?
e: Herschel crater is 130 km across on a moon only 396 km in diameter. The impact that created it must have nearly destroyed Mimas entirely. The resemblance to the Death Star is coincidental — Star Wars was released in 1977, but Herschel crater was not discovered until Voyager 1 imaged Mimas in 1980.

s: Space smells like seared steak, welding fumes, and gunpowder according to astronauts who have sniffed their suits after spacewalks.
q: What do astronauts say space smells like?
e: Astronauts cannot smell space directly, but after EVAs they notice a distinct metallic, charred odor clinging to their suits and equipment. Scientists believe this comes from dying stars releasing polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons — the same compounds produced when meat is grilled.

s: The Milky Way and Andromeda galaxy are on a collision course and will merge in about 4.5 billion years — but individual star collisions will be extremely rare because of the vast distances between stars.
q: When the Milky Way and Andromeda collide, what will happen to individual stars?
a: Very few will actually collide due to vast distances between them
d: Nearly all stars will collide with each other immediately
The collision will cause the galaxies to explode into separate fragments
Most stars will escape the merged galaxy entirely
Individual stars will merge into larger supergiants
The collision will destroy the majority of stars
Stars will be compressed into a neutron star cluster
e: Despite containing hundreds of billions of stars each, the space between them is so enormous that the two galaxies will pass through each other like two swarms of bees. The resulting merged galaxy, sometimes called 'Milkomeda,' will likely be a giant elliptical galaxy.

s: A region of space called the Bootes Void is so empty that it spans 330 million light-years across and contains only about 60 galaxies — where it should contain about 2,000.
q: What makes the Bootes Void unusual?
a: It contains far fewer galaxies than expected
d: It is a region of intense radiation that kills all galaxies
It contains far more galaxies than expected
It is a blackhole that has consumed all galaxies within it
It is named after an ancient astronomer
It contains only stellar remnants, no active stars
It is expanding at faster than light speed
e: Discovered in 1981, the Bootes Void is one of the largest known voids in the universe. If the Milky Way were at the center of this void, we would not have discovered the existence of other galaxies until the 1960s because they would have been too far away for earlier telescopes.

s: Jupiter's Great Red Spot is shrinking — it was once wide enough to fit three Earths side by side, but has been getting smaller since the 1800s and now fits only about one Earth.
q: How has Jupiter's Great Red Spot changed over time?
d: It has been slowly growing in size
It has remained the same size for millions of years
It is now invisible from telescopes
e: First definitively observed in the 1830s, the Great Red Spot has been continuously monitored and is visibly shrinking. At its peak, it was about 40,000 km wide; it is now roughly 16,000 km across. Scientists are not certain why it is shrinking or whether it will eventually disappear.

s: There is a planet made largely of diamond — 55 Cancri e is a super-Earth where extreme pressure likely compressed its carbon-rich interior into a layer of crystalline diamond.
q: Which exoplanet is believed to contain a massive layer of diamond?
e: 55 Cancri e orbits so close to its star that a year lasts just 18 hours and its surface temperature reaches 2,400°C. Studies suggest up to a third of the planet's mass could be diamond, making it worth roughly 26.9 nonillion dollars — far more than the entire global economy.

s: Astronauts grow up to 5 cm taller in space because without gravity compressing them, the discs between their vertebrae expand.
q: How much taller can astronauts grow while in space?
d: They remain exactly the same height
e: The spinal column contains gel-filled discs that act as cushions between vertebrae. On Earth, gravity compresses them throughout the day. In microgravity, these discs expand freely, temporarily adding height. Astronauts shrink back to normal within days of returning to Earth.

s: There is a storm on Neptune called the Great Dark Spot where winds reach 2,100 km/h — the fastest winds ever recorded on any planet in the solar system.
q: Which planet has the fastest recorded winds in the solar system?
e: Neptune's winds far exceed anything on Earth, where the strongest recorded gust was about 407 km/h. Despite being the farthest major planet from the Sun, Neptune's atmosphere is extremely dynamic. Scientists believe internal heat drives these supersonic winds, since Neptune radiates 2.6 times more energy than it receives from the Sun.

s: The Sun loses about 4 million tonnes of mass every second as it converts hydrogen into helium through nuclear fusion — and it has been doing this for 4.6 billion years.
q: How much mass does the Sun lose every second through nuclear fusion?
e: Despite this enormous rate of mass loss, the Sun has only consumed about 0.03% of its total mass over its entire lifetime. It still has enough hydrogen fuel to keep burning for another 5 billion years before expanding into a red giant and eventually becoming a white dwarf.

s: Pluto's largest moon Charon is so big relative to Pluto that they actually orbit each other around a point in empty space — making them the solar system's only recognized double dwarf planet system.
q: What makes the Pluto-Charon system unique in the solar system?
a: They orbit a point between them in empty space
d: Charon orbits inside Pluto like normal moons
Pluto has three moons that orbit a barycenter
They are tidally locked but Charon orbits inside Pluto
They revolve around the Sun independently
Charon orbits much closer to Pluto than other moons
They are not gravitationally bound to each other
e: Most moons orbit a point inside their parent planet (the barycenter). But Charon is about half Pluto's diameter, so their shared center of gravity lies in the space between them. They are tidally locked, always showing the same face to each other — like two dancers spinning hand in hand.

s: Gamma-ray bursts are the most energetic explosions in the universe — a single burst can release more energy in 10 seconds than the Sun will emit in its entire 10-billion-year lifetime.
q: How much energy can a gamma-ray burst release in 10 seconds?
a: More than the Sun will emit in its entire lifetime
d: About the same as the Sun's total lifetime energy
More than a human lifetime of sunlight
Less energy than a solar flare
More than all nuclear weapons combined
Just slightly more than the Sun per year
e: These cosmic cataclysms come in two types: short bursts from neutron star mergers and long bursts from massive star collapses. If one occurred within about 6,000 light-years of Earth and was aimed at us, it could strip away the ozone layer and trigger a mass extinction.

s: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain virtually unchanged for millions of years because there is no wind, rain, or weather to erode them.
q: Why will the Apollo astronauts' footprints last millions of years on the Moon?
a: There is no atmosphere or weather to erode them
d: The Moon has constant dust storms that erase footprints
Micrometeorites will destroy them within days
The lunar surface is constantly shifting
Temperature fluctuations cause the surface to shatter
e: Without an atmosphere, the Moon has no weathering processes. The only forces that slowly degrade the footprints are micrometeorite impacts and solar radiation, which work on timescales of tens of millions of years. Barring a direct meteorite hit, Neil Armstrong's boot print could outlast human civilization.

s: The planet HD 189733 b has winds of 8,700 km/h and rains molten glass sideways — the glass gets its blue color from silicate particles in the atmosphere.
q: What rains from the sky on exoplanet HD 189733 b?
a: Molten glass driven sideways by extreme winds
e: This hot Jupiter, 63 light-years away, looks like a beautiful deep blue marble from space, similar to Earth. But the resemblance ends there — its blue color comes from silicate (glass) particles in the atmosphere that scatter blue light, and extreme temperatures vaporize these silicates into clouds that rain glass at hypersonic speeds.

s: The largest known structure in the observable universe is the Hercules-Corona Borealis Great Wall — a filament of galaxies stretching about 10 billion light-years across.
q: What is the largest known structure in the observable universe?
e: Discovered in 2013 through gamma-ray burst mapping, this cosmic megastructure is so large it challenges cosmological models that predict the universe should look uniform at such scales. Light from one end would take 10 billion years to reach the other — comparable to the age of the universe itself.

s: Time passes measurably faster on the ISS than on Earth's surface — after a year in orbit, astronauts return about 0.01 seconds younger than they would have been on the ground.
q: After a year on the ISS, how do astronauts' clocks compare to clocks on Earth?
d: Astronauts' clocks run exactly the same speed as Earth
Astronauts gain about 0.01 seconds per year
Time passes 1000 times slower on the ISS
Astronauts don't experience any time dilation
Astronauts age 1 second more per year
The effect cancels out completely at ISS altitude
e: Two effects of Einstein's relativity compete here: speed slows time (special relativity), but weaker gravity speeds it up (general relativity). On the ISS, the speed effect wins slightly, so astronauts age a tiny bit less. GPS satellites experience the opposite — their clocks run faster due to weaker gravity at higher orbits, requiring daily corrections of 38 microseconds.

s: Vanguard SLV-1 was America's first failed satellite launch attempt in 1957.
q: What was Vanguard SLV-1's primary outcome?
e: America's embarrassing first satellite failure sparked the Space Race!

s: Vanguard SLV-3 was an unsuccessful rocket launch attempt in space exploration history.
q: What was Vanguard SLV-3?
e: Early space race failures taught crucial lessons for future successes!

s: Vanguard SLV-5 was a failed rocket launch by the United States.
q: What was Vanguard SLV-5?
e: Early space race failures pushed innovation!

s: Vanguard SLV-6 was a failed American rocket launch attempt.
q: What was Vanguard SLV-6?
e: Early space race failures taught engineers critical lessons!
